questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "I might stay home tonight — ___"
questions.answer: I'm not sure yet.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "She will definitely get the job. ___"
questions.answer: She is completely certain about it.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "He probably won't come to the party ___"
questions.answer: because he is very busy this week.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "You could find a cheaper flat ___"
questions.answer: if you look outside the city centre.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "They will probably move abroad ___"
questions.answer: but they haven't decided for sure.

questions.prompt: I'm not sure about my plans — I _____ go to the beach if the weather is good.
questions.answer: might — Use 'might', 'may' or 'could' for something possible but uncertain.

questions.prompt: She has studied really hard, so she will _____ pass the exam.
questions.answer: definitely — Both 'definitely' and 'probably' work, since there's not enough context.

questions.prompt: I _____ won't have enough money to travel this summer.
questions.answer: probably — Both 'definitely' and 'probably' work, since there's not enough context.

questions.prompt: He _____ get a promotion soon — his boss really likes him.
questions.answer: will probably — Use 'will probably' for near certainty in a positive sentence.

questions.prompt: They _____ move to another city, but they haven't decided yet.
questions.answer: might — Use 'might', 'may' or 'could' when something is just a possibility.

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "She might to call you later this evening."
questions.answer: No 'to' after modal verbs: 'She might call you later.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "I definitely will pass the exam — I studied all week."
questions.answer: 'Definitely' comes after 'will': 'I will definitely pass the exam.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "He will probably not comes to the meeting tomorrow."
questions.answer: After 'will', use the base verb: 'He will probably not come' or 'He probably won't come.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "They could to find a cheaper place if they look online."
questions.answer: No 'to' after modal verbs: 'They could find a cheaper place.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "He works very hard, so he will pass definitely."
questions.answer: 'Definitely' must come between 'will' and the verb: 'he will definitely pass.'
I might be late.
She will definitely pass.
He probably won't come.
You could find a better job.
They will probably move next year.
